name: Arena Allocation
summary: Bump-pointer allocation from a contiguous memory region; freed in bulk when a phase ends. The dominant memory-management strategy in compilers because AST and IR nodes share phase-aligned lifetimes.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Arena (bump) allocation is the dominant memory management strategy in compiler frontends and middle-ends. The pattern: allocate a large contiguous region, bump a pointer forward for each allocation, never free individual objects. When a compilation phase ends, free the entire arena at once.

This is acceptable in compilers because AST nodes, IR instructions, and type objects all have the same lifetime — they live for one compilation phase and die together. Per-object deallocation would be unnecessary work; bulk free at phase boundaries is dramatically cheaper.

Language-facing allocator API models (explicit allocator parameters, context allocators, temp allocators, allocator toolkits) are a separate memory-management concern. This record covers arenas as an internal compiler implementation technique.

sections.title: Bump-pointer mechanism
sections.content:
Allocation reduces to a pointer bump plus an alignment check — typically about 2 ns. There is no per-object deallocation, no free list, no fragmentation. The trade-off is that objects in the arena cannot be individually freed, which is acceptable when all allocated objects share a compilation-phase lifetime.

The `bump-scope` crate extends bump allocation with allocation scopes/checkpoints: callers can reset the arena to a previous point, freeing everything allocated after that point. This supports nested phases — parse a function body, optimize it, emit code, reset the arena for the next function — without giving up the bulk-free property.

Arenas also enable pointer-free representations: instead of storing pointers to children, store 32-bit offsets into the arena. Indices halve the per-edge cost compared to 64-bit pointers, save substantial memory on large ASTs, and make serialization trivial — the arena bytes are the serialized form. This is the same approach data-oriented design uses in ECS game engines and that flat-AST representations adopt.

sections.title: Production usage
sections.content:
Rust's `bumpalo` crate is the canonical implementation in the Rust ecosystem. Many hand-written C and C++ parsers use `mmap`-backed arenas with the same pattern. Most compiler frontends — rustc, Clang, GCC, Zig, Swift's parser — allocate ASTs and IR nodes through some form of arena rather than the general allocator.

The scoped variant, supporting reset to checkpoints, is widely used for pass-local scratch allocations: a pass allocates working data, runs, and resets the arena before the next pass starts. This avoids both per-object teardown and any ambient global allocator pressure during the pass.

Many compilers' "AST" type is structurally an arena plus an entry point — there is no separate AST type at all. The arena is the AST.

sections.title: Relation to language-level allocator APIs
sections.content:
Arena allocation as a compiler internal is distinct from arena allocation as a language feature. Languages such as Zig, Odin, C3, Jai, and Beef expose explicit allocator parameters or context allocators that let user code request arena behavior — this is a memory-management API design concern, not a compiler implementation concern.

The two interact when the language's compiler is itself written in the language and uses its own allocator API for compiler-internal allocations (Zig's compiler is the notable example). Otherwise, the compiler's choice of internal arena allocator is independent of how user code manages memory.
